content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 607 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance/Administration Executive/Legal Enforcement/Conflict of Interest
916 322 5660 322 5001 322 6441

March 22, 1977

John B. Rudquist
Cavalero, Bray, Shumway & Geiger
404 Bank of Stockton Building
311 East Main Street
Stockton, California 95202

Dear Mr. Rudquist:

Thank you for your letter of March 10, 1977, raising a number of
specific inquiries concerning application of the Political Reform Act
to your clients. Initially, you refer to new Government Code Section
87206.5 which excludes the principal residence of the filer from dis-
closure when an interest in real property is required to be disclosed,
and you ask whether it is proper in advising your Article 3 filers
to inform them that they need not disclose a parcel of real property
upon which they maintain a principal residence. It is quite correct
to inform your Article 3 filers that they need not disclose their
principal residence when real property is required to be disclosed
provided, however, that the residence does not constitute any other
type of reportable interest, such as a reportable investment, report-
able income, or reportable interest in real property.

You next point out that some of your clients have their principal
residence on parcels of property comprised of 15, 25, 75 or more acres
and you ask whether the entire parcel may be excluded from disclosure.
When real property consists of land in addition to the portion used
as part of the principal residence, all such additional property is
disclosable.

You next ask how a parcel of property consisting in part of one's
principal residence, ought to be divided and for purposes of determining
equity value, how the value of the residence can be separated from the
value of the entire parcel when both jointly are encumbered with a
deed of trust. In the usual residential context, both the principal
residence and the lot or real property on which the principal resi-
dence is located, are excluded from disclosure as explained above.

In a non-residential context, while we have no mathematical
formula for computing precisely how the real property is to be div-
ided for purposes of disclosure and non-disclosure, it would seem
reasonable to exclude, that portion of property, used as part of

John E. Rudquist
page 2
March 22, 1977

the principal residence. Property in excess of this, would be required
to be disclosed. The Commission has not ruled on the appropriate size
of the property to be excluded along with the principal residence.
But in a rural setting, it would not seem unreasonable to exclude a
full acre. Where, however, all but a small portion of property on
which the principal residence is located, is used for farming or other
non-residential purposes, then these factual circumstances would dic-
tate that all property, except the small portion on which the prin-
cipal residence is located, would have to be disclosed. With regard
to separating the value of the residence from the value of the re-
maining portion of the entire parcel, it would seem quite reasonable
to simply assign a percentage of the total to the residence and that
portion of the parcel on which the residence is situated. Precisely
how these computations are to be made would be left to the individual
concerned taking into consideration actual use of the property, the
fair market value of residences in the area, zoning, etcetera.

You raise an additional interesting question. A corporation is
wholly owned by the filer and his wife. The corporation leases the
principal residence [presumably with surrounding farmland] of the
filer and his wife and farms it as part of its corporate operation.
Government Code Section 87206(c) would, under these factual circum-
stances, require disclosure of the interests in real property of the
corporation. In light of Government Code Section 87206.5, however,
which in substance has been incorporated into the draft of most
Conflicts of Interests Codes, you ask whether disclosure of this real
property, which is the principal residence of the filer, would have
to be disclosed. The answer is yes, this interest in real property
would have to be disclosed because it constitutes a reportable invest-
ment in addition to being a principal residence of the filer. In
reviewing Government Code Section 87206.5, you will note that the
exception is only for the principal residence of the filer. In your
fact situation the farm land surrounding the residence is leased for
farming by the corporation and this would have to be disclosed.

As you are probably aware, persons with duties under the Political
Reform Act of 1974, as amended, may request advice or an opinion from
the Commission with regard to those duties. Both the Act and the regu-
lations of the Commission provide immunization from sanctions under the
Act to a person relying upon an opinion or advice from the Commission
provided, however, that the material facts are as stated in the
request for an opinion or advice. I would like to point out, however,
that the protections afforded by the Act and the regulations extend
only to the person identified in the request for an opinion or advice.
The last portion of your letter lists six specific types of interests
and asks whether they are included within the definition of "interest
in real property." While you may have individual clients who have
particular concerns with regard to some of these questions, the questions

John B. Rudquist
page 3
March 22, 1977

raised are hypothetical questions. You do not identify any of the clients
on whose behalf they are being asked nor do you provide any factual
circumstances in which these questions arise. Accordingly, pursuant
to our regulations, your request for advice with regard to each of these
specific types of interest, is being denied. Should you have specific
factual circumstances and identify the particular client on whose behalf
these questions are being asked, we will be glad to reconsider this
response.

Sincerely,

[Signature]
Edgar A. Kerry
Staff Attorney
Conflicts of Interest Division
(916) 322-6444

EAK:mfn
